title: Can LLMs Rank the Harmfulness of Smaller LLMs? We are Not There Yet
authors: Berk Atil  Vipul Gupta  Sarkar Snigdha Sarathi Das  Rebecca J. PassonneauPenn State University{bka5352,vkg5164,sfd5525,rjp49}@psu.edu
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have become ubiquitous, thus it is important to understand their risks and limitations. Smaller LLMs can be deployed where compute resources are constrained, such as edge devices, but with different propensity to generate harmful output. Mitigation of LLM harm typically depends on annotating the harmfulness of LLM output, which is expensive to collect from humans. This work studies two questions: How do smaller LLMs rank regarding generation of harmful content? How well can larger LLMs annotate harmfulness? We prompt three small LLMs to elicit harmful content of various types, such as discriminatory language, offensive content, privacy invasion, or negative influence, and collect human rankings of their outputs. Then, we evaluate three state-of-the-art large LLMs on their ability to annotate the harmfulness of these responses. We find that the smaller models differ with respect to harmfulness. We also find that large LLMs show low to moderate agreement with humans. These findings underline the need for further work on harm mitigation in LLMs.

paragraphs: Thanks to the high performance of large language models (LLMs) on diverse tasks such as question answering, summarization, and sentiment classification, they are increasingly ubiquitous. Yet there remains the risk of unpredictable generation of undesirable outputs that fall under the general category of “harm”. Harm includes information about execution of dangerous activities, discrimination, stereotyping, privacy violations, offensive language, and hate speech Kumar et al. (2023). Recent work proposes mitigation methods for use at inference-time or training-time Li et al. (2024); Ko et al. (2024); Khondaker et al. (2024); Niu et al. (2024); Wang et al. (2024). Much of this work focuses on smaller LLMs (≤\leq 10B parameters), because of their convenience for users with fewer compute resources Shakhadri et al. (2024), for use on edge devices Xu et al. (2024), or to minimize adverse effects on the environment Schwartz et al. (2020). There is also the potential for harm mitigation methods developed on smaller LLMs to inform harm mitigation in larger LLMs. Most of this work aims for quantitative measures, such as the probability of generating harmful output, which faces numerous challenges. We take an alternative approach of ranking harmfulness of models, based on human comparisons among model outputs when they are given the same prompts. Our approach has the extra benefit of yielding a dataset of preference judgements, which could support harm mitigation methods like RLHF or direct optimization (DPO).

Our approach to collecting data that leads to a means to ranking LLMs for harmfulness depends on three steps: (1) elicitation of harmful output from different LLMs; (2) rating the relative harmfulness of outputs from different models; (3) deriving an overall ranking of LLMs. In this paper, we use a previously collected dataset of human interactions with LLMs with harmful outputs for human prompts to serve as “harm triggers.” We then use our harm triggers to elicit harmful output. We also release the dataset consisting of harm-triggering prompts and human ratings of model responses.

We also study whether larger and more powerful LLMs with strong reasoning skills, such as GPT4o OpenAI (2024) or Sonnet Anthropic (2024), can reliably evaluate the harmfulness of smaller LLMs. At best, we find moderate agreement between larger LLMs and humans. This demonstrates the need for further research into LLMs for data annotation in subjective tasks. Our contributions are:

1. We collect a dataset of harm-triggering prompts and human ratings of the relative harmfulness of responses to those prompts generated by three smaller (≤\leq 10B) LLMs.111URL will be provided if paper is accepted.
2. We use human ratings on the output triples to rank the smaller models.
3. We evaluate agreement of similar ratings from large LLMs with the human ratings.
Alongside a discussion of what is meant by LLM harmfulness, this section covers two distinct strands of related work: measuring types of harm in LLMs, and LLMs for diverse annotation tasks.

Diverse undesirable LLM outputs, from toxic language to privacy invasion, have been discussed in the observed Banko et al. (2020). Here we review the ones we include in our definition of “harm.” Toxic content can be elicited from both generative Deshpande et al. (2023) and masked LLMs Ousidhoum et al. (2021). To measure toxic or hateful language, some use APIs such as PerspectiveAPI Lees et al. (2022) or HateBERT Caselli et al. (2021). OpenAI (2024) report that GPT4 produces toxic content 0.78% of the time, versus 6.48% in GPT3.5. Dubey et al. (2024) report that llama3-70B produces harmful content 5% of the time, compared to 3% in the 405B model. Instead of reporting an absolute rate, we focus on relative harmfulness of different LLMs.

The first category of harm we consider is social stereotyping and bias. LLMs can perpetuate social bias based on gender, race, religion etc. Lin et al. (2022); Bender et al. (2021); Field et al. (2021); Gupta et al. (2024); Andriushchenko et al. (2024); Mazeika et al. (2024). This can marginalize these groups more, and results in less fair model performance. Guo et al. (2024) designed a competition to elicit biased output from LLMs to assess the perception of bias from non-expert users. We also intentionally elicit harmful output, going beyond social bias.

Our second category of harm is offensiveness and toxicity, which lacks an established definition due to its greater subjectivity Dev et al. (2022); Korre et al. (2023). We include hate speech (HS) and abusive language as toxic content. HS can be defined as expressions of offensive and discriminatory discourse towards a group or an individual based on characteristics such as race, religion, nationality, or other group characteristics John (2000); Jahan and Oussalah (2023); Basile et al. (2019); Davidson et al. (2017). It includes racism, negative stereotyping, and sexist language. On the other hand, abusive language is content with inappropriate words such as profanity or disrespectful terms. It also includes psychological threats such as humiliation.

Harm can be implicit, such as privacy invasion where there is leakage of personal information. This includes social security numbers, phone numbers, or bank account information Carlini et al. (2021); Brown et al. (2022).

For data annotation, LLMs can replace humans for some tasks, with gains in efficiency and economy Tan et al. (2024). They have been used for sociological annotations such as for classification of stance, bots or humor Ziems et al. (2024); Zhu et al. (2023). For tasks such as topic and frame detection or sentence segmentation they can surpass crowd-workers He et al. (2024); Gilardi et al. (2023). Some have argued that human-LLM collaboration results in more reliable annotation He et al. (2024); Zhang et al. (2023); Kim et al. (2024). In addition to more objective tasks, they have been applied to subjective annotations such as offensiveness and abusiveness Pavlovic and Poesio (2024); Zhu et al. (2023); He et al. (2023), or to rank outputs from different LLMs based on helpfulness, accuracy, or relevance Zheng et al. (2023); Lin et al. (2024); Dubois et al. (2024). These works tend to focus on human-large LLM interactions, whereas we focus on single-turn responses from smaller LLMs. We inspire from Zheng et al. (2023) but we only measure harm instead of overall performance. Plus, we use 3 LLMs to evaluate smaller LLMs.

The data was collected in a graduate-level NLP course in Spring 2024 at a public university, from 17 pairs of students. We randomly sampled 60 different prompts for each student pair (1,020 total human prompts) from the HH-RLHF dataset of human-LLM interactions Bai et al. (2022). We selected three LLMs that were open-source, reasonably sized to fit a single GPU, and among the currently best performing models: Mistral 7B-Instruct Jiang et al. (2023), MPT 7B-Instruct Team (2023), and StableLM-tuned-alpha-7B Taori et al. (2023). Students were instructed to use the same prompts for the three models, and to rate the harmfulness of the three responses, with 0 for not harmful, and harmfulness from 1 to 3 (most)

Since harm or offensive language is subjective, there is no established definition in the literature. However, for better consistency, we provided students with the following criteria: (1) Including language that discriminates against, marginalizes, or shows bias towards particular groups based on race, gender, religion, nationality, sexual orientation, or any other group characteristic; (2) offensive content, including text that is vulgar, violent, sexually explicit, or otherwise offensive to individuals or groups; (3) encouraging or glorifying violence, self-harm, substance abuse, or activities that are illegal, immoral, or unethical; (4) privacy invasion, meaning personal information leakage; (5) encouraging negative social behavior such as bullying, harassment, or spreading hate speech. We intentionally excluded misinformation or factuality errors because this requires external knowledge.

subsections.title: 3.1 Data Cleaning
paragraphs: Because there were some inconsistencies in the data returned by the students, we standardized the data format. We also discarded cases where the model generated no output. These steps left us with 984 distinct prompts (96.5% of the original), and three labeled responses for each prompt, including 0. To measure the quality of student annotations, two experts (co-authors) annotated 5 examples from each student pair.

Each model produced harmful output, sometimes in the extreme. Although all prompts were in English, some StableLM outputs were in Korean, suggesting exposure to more Korean in its training data. Repetitiveness and nonsense responses were common for all models, but less so for Mistral, suggesting a potential issue with fine-tuning. Furthermore, Mistral output was more detailed and user-oriented, even including detailed guidance for harmful actions. In addition, we observed output similar to content present in online websites, such as inclusion of usernames from social media platforms such as Reddit, which is related to the issue of leakage of private information. Below we present the ranking and automated labeling results.

subsections.title: 4.1 Quantitative Ranking Results
paragraphs: During data collection, none of the models were restricted as to the output length. Interestingly, sometimes very long outputs of up to 2000 words were generated, usually consisting largely of repetitions and nonsense responses. Table 1 shows the min, median, and max output lengths for each model across the 984 prompts (cf. Appendix Figure 4 for length plots). While StableLM generated the longest output, it has the lowest median length.

Mistral, MPT, and StableLM produced harmful content 58%, 61%, and 49% of the time, respectively, in response to our harm triggers. Figure 1, which excludes examples with at least one 0 rating, shows that StableLM produced the least harmful output . Mistral had the most 3s, although Mistral and MPT had similar average ratings; StableLM had the lowest rating. Figure 2 gives the 95% confidence intervals for the average ratings of each model.

Besides looking at the overall harmfulness and ratings of the models, we also compare them pairwise. Figure 3 shows that StableLM frequently wins (is less harmful) over the other two; between the other two, there is no clear winner. This is consistent with the previous observation that StableLM produces a lower proportion of harmful output.

subsections.title: 4.2 Automated Harm Detection
paragraphs: To investigate whether larger LLMs can rate models’ harmfulness similarly to humans, we prompt three state-of-the-art LLMs: GPT4o OpenAI (2024), Claude3.5 Sonnet Anthropic (2024), and LLama3.1 70b-instruct Dubey et al. (2024). We provide the same definition of harm we provided to students, and use zero-shot and zero-shot CoT Wei et al. (2022). The exact prompts can be found in Tables 4 and 5 of the Appendix.

The relative ratings in our data range from 0 (non-harmful) to 3 (the most harmful). Note that when there is at least one non-harmful response, the meaning of the relative values changes. For example, consider the examples “1,2,31,2,3” and “0,0,10,0,1”: “1” means the least harmful in the first example but the most harmful in the latter. Due to the relative nature of the labels, Pearson is not applicable, and rank correlation coefficients are not suitable because they are not defined if the array is constant (e.g., all 0s). Inter-annotator agreement metrics such as Krippendorf’s alpha Krippendorff (2011), which rely on frequency of label values, are also not applicable. We use Rank-Biased Overlap (RBO) Webber et al. (2010). RBO measures the similarity of indefinite rankings, where some items might not be given a rank, top elements are more important than the tail, and the decision to truncate the ranking at any depth is arbitrary. RBO can handle ties, and can measure the similarity between different length arrays, so it fits well to our case. RBO provides a value between 0 and 1 where 1 means perfect similarity, calculated as follows:

The parameter pp adjusts the importance of top rankings relative to the tails; dd represents a position/depth in the ranking; AdA_{d}, the agreement at depth dd, is calculated as the intersection of the rankings at depth dd divided by dd. We set pp close to 1, so each label value is equally important.

Table 2 shows the RBO scores for paired comparisons of the three models with each other and the students, on outputs from zero-shot versions of the 984 prompts.222CoT prompting had inconsistent results. Sonnet is most similar to the students, while Llama-70b is the least so. Models’ RBO scores are more similar to each other than to the students, despite coming from different LLM families. To evaluate the quality of the student annotations, Table 3 reports RBO scores on the subset of data that experts annotated (85 prompts). At an RBO of 0.66, students correlate the most with experts, and Sonnet is the closest model (RBO=0.57). Score differences on the subset are 5% or less except for students vs. Sonnet (12% increase) and GPT vs. Llama (9% drop). The RBO scores indicate that LLMs ratings are not on a par with humans.

While alignment methods applied to larger LLMs have led to relatively low rates of harmfulness, smaller LLMs can also benefit from harm mitigation. Our results indicate that harm triggers applied to small LLMs (≤\leq 10B) can lead to harmful outputs over half the time. Our dataset of 984 harm triggers and rating triples for three models’ outputs has potential use for harm mitigation. We also assessed large LLM harmfulness ranking as a means of automating the collection of harmfulness preference data. However, pairwise RBO scores show that harmfulness ratings from large LLMs are not commensurate with human ratings.

We experiment with three small LLMs and there are stronger LLMs such as Llama3.1-8b. We also did not apply any sophisticated methods to elicit harmful content because we would like to simulate an average user. Furthermore, we use three SOTA LLMs as judges with two different prompting. There might be better models or prompts that can improve the correlation of LLMs with humans.
